Name: -1_medication_psychiatric_like_use
Representative_Docs:
['This May, during Mental Health Awareness Month, we are highlighting an important distinction: the difference between mental health and mental health conditions. 

Many people think these terms mean the same thing, but they don't. Understanding the distinction can help us take better care of ourselves and support others more effectively.

Here is the difference:

💚Mental health is universal - Everyone has mental health. It includes your emotional, psychological, and social well-being and affects how you think, feel, and act in daily life.

💚Mental health conditions are specific - These are diagnosable disorders like depression, anxiety, or post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). More than 50% of people will receive a mental health diagnosis at some point.

💚Good mental health and mental illness can coexist - Many people living with mental health conditions have periods of strong mental well-being through treatment, support, and self-care.

💚Poor mental health does not always mean mental illness - You can experience poor mental health due to stress, burnout, grief, or life transitions without having a diagnosable condition.

💚Simple ways to care for your mental health - Find coping skills that work for you. These could include journaling, deep breathing, moving your body, or spending time with loved ones.

💚Professional support is available - Therapy can benefit anyone, not just those with diagnosed conditions. 

Support for mental health is a critical Michigan public policy issue that the Citizens Research Council has covered extensively. You can find our work here: www.crcmich.org -- write "mental health" in the search engine.

If you need support, please reach out to a health care professional. Help is available, and you're not alone.', 'The Role of Pharmacists in Mental Health Care (Part 1): Closing Psychiatric Pharmacotherapy Gaps


It would be remiss not to address mental health given that May is Mental Health Awareness Month.

Mental health needs continue to rise while workforce shortages persist across healthcare. Yet one important part of the solution is often underrecognized:
Pharmacists.

Psychiatric pharmacists and other pharmacists practicing within mental health settings are helping close critical care gaps through specialized expertise in psychiatric pharmacotherapy.

Psychiatric medication management is rarely as simple as "prescribe and continue."

Many patients require:

• Careful medication selection
• Monitoring for efficacy and adverse effects
• Management of drug interactions
• Titration and optimization
• Addressing adherence challenges
• Identification of medication-related causes of psychiatric symptoms
• Education and shared decision-making

This is where psychiatric pharmacists provide substantial value.

Psychiatric pharmacists frequently collaborate with psychiatrists and interdisciplinary teams to:

✓ Recommend, interpret, and help operationalize pharmacogenomic testing when appropriate
✓ Facilitate evidence-based nonpharmacological interventions such as lifestyle and behavioral interventions, psychotherapy, and social or functional support
✓ Optimize psychiatric pharmacotherapy
✓ Manage pharmacotherapy-related adverse effects
✓ Support treatment-resistant conditions
✓ Monitor and manage metabolic risks associated with psychotropic medications
✓ Manage transitions of care
✓ Improve medication adherence and tolerability

Beyond medication expertise, they often serve as translators between complex pharmacotherapy and patient understanding.

We have an increasingly overburdened mental health system where access to psychiatrists and other mental health clinicians is limited, making pharmacist expertise even more valuable.

But mental health care is not solely about expanding personnel numbers.

It is also about maximizing the expertise of the professionals already embedded within our systems.

For many patients, the difference between treatment failure and treatment success is not simply access to medication, but access to medication optimization.

No mental health workforce discussion is complete without recognizing pharmaceutical care because medication optimization often influences the highly variable outcomes seen within this complex patient population.

Pharmacists do not simply support mental health care. They expand its reach, strengthen its capacity, and are an important part of the solution.

#MentalHealthAwarenessMonth #PsychiatricPharmacy #MentalHealth #Pharmacists #HealthcareInnovation #PatientCare #BehavioralHealth #MedicationManagement', 'Across public health, mental health, outdoor recreation, and policy spaces, there is growing recognition that improving wellbeing in this country will require us to think differently about prevention, connection, and care.

Next week, I’ll join Senator Michael Bennet, Catherine Price, and Jessica (Wahl) Turner at Outside Days for a conversation on:
“The Outdoor Prescription: Health, Access, and Public Policy.”

Outdoor Recreation Roundtable’s Health Vision is helping push that conversation forward by positioning outdoor access and recreation as part of America’s broader health infrastructure. And Senator Michael Bennet’s work on expanding access to mental and behavioral healthcare calls us to “reconsider how we can bring local communities into the continuum of care.” 

Together, these ideas reflect a growing understanding that healing cannot rest solely inside clinical systems.

At Boyz N The Wood, we’ve witnessed firsthand how nature, brotherhood, and trusted community can create conditions for healing, especially for Black men navigating stress, isolation, and disconnection.

That experience has led us to larger questions:
\xa0• What would it look like to equip local communities as key components in our continuum of care?\xa0
\xa0• What would it look like for government, healthcare, philanthropy, outdoor recreation, and public agencies to invest in that infrastructure together?

These are not just theoretical questions for us. We’re asking and helping to shape the answer through our Colorado Communities of Care pilot, a place-based effort exploring how outdoor access, mental health support, research, and community leadership can work together as part of a broader health ecosystem.

This conversation is growing across sectors. Excited to both discuss the idea on the panel, and work on bringing it to life.']

Name: 0_digital_data_article_media
Representative_Docs:
['A risk assessment of AI mental health apps from Common Sense Media and Brainstorm: The Stanford Lab for Mental Health Innovation was released today. Five apps were evaluated. Two vanished from app stores mid-review with no notice and no transition support for active users. One was rated Unacceptable Risk for teen safety. 

Sonar was described as “the standard every product in this space should be held to.” 

The criteria seem obvious. Principles including: Keep kids safe. Be trustworthy. Support human connection. What the report documents is that several products made choices that conflict with those values.

What we built at Sonar is a model that makes safe, effective, human-led support scalable through AI. Every youth-facing message is reviewed and sent by a trained, human Wellbeing Coach. The AI supports coaches so they can reach more youth, respond faster, and never miss a signal. That is the design choice that this assessment evaluated.

The report also notes that Sonar has no peer-reviewed studies yet. This is true. We have outcomes data we and our partners find encouraging, and we are actively working with academic partners on controlled trials. We look forward to sharing those studies when finalized.

To the schools, community behavioral health clinics, and health systems that have trusted us, and to the young people, clinicians, and families within them, thank you. 25,000+ young people across nine states are receiving coaching from real people because of that trust.

As a founder and builder in this space, I welcome analyses like this one, and not just because the findings reflect well on Sonar. It is not a victory to be "Minimally Risky." It is a victory for all of us to have transparent, objective standards applied uniformly to an unprecedented and unregulated space where real human lives are at stake.', 'This week, a roundtable on digital mental health was held in Colombo, co-hosted by the Prime Minister’s Office and the eMental Health International Collaborative. This brought together clinicians and community practitioners, persons with lived experience of MH conditions, researchers, innovation and industry representatives, policy makers and govt officials to discuss the state of digital mental health in Sri Lanka and its future. 

In her keynote address, PM Dr.Harini Amarasuriya spoke about the need to prioritise the following in digital mental health innovation for Sri Lanka: Accessibility and Equity, Quality and Safety, Participation of persons with lived experience, Cost-Effectiveness and Integration with existing systems for Scalability and Sustainability. She also expressed the need for, “regulatory frameworks that encourage innovation while ensuring efficacy, safety, data privacy, security, and other ethical considerations”.

These considerations were echoed in the discussions that followed in small group exchanges between the diverse participants - many of whom were meeting  for the first time, despite their overlapping interests in the area. There were many connections made and offers of support were made both bilaterally between participants and to the wider group to help take both conversation and actions forward.

During the roundtable, we also had the opportunity to hear Senator Katherine (Kathy) Hay ICD.D of Canada speak about the principles and approaches that underpinned the transformation of the Kids Help Phone services that she led over the past 7 years, and also hear Prof Andrew Greenshaw and Prof Anil Thapliyal brief us on the ongoing rapid developments in digital mental health globally.

I’m very hopeful for what will come from this meeting and the conversation that has started amongst diverse stakeholders who will need to work together to advance digital mental health in SL. 

If you are interested in getting engaged, stay tuned for updates over the next weeks and months - and do share what you are doing in this area!', '🧠 What’s New in Digital Mental Health Research\xa0
To stay current with digital mental health research, I’ve identified a handful of recent studies that offer practical and timely insights for anyone working at the intersection of mental health, technology, and care delivery, particularly for youth and individuals with complex clinical needs. Here are a few that stood out:

📍 1. Early engagement matters
🔗 https://lnkd.in/g37_AKSh
Among young people using an unguided anxiety self-help app, those who completed more tasks early on were more likely to keep using the app, regardless of their initial anxiety severity.
Why it matters:
Focusing on onboarding and early interaction, through personalization, reminders, or gamification, can improve long-term engagement and outcomes.

📍 2. Combining digital tools with face-to-face therapy improves results
🔗 https://lnkd.in/gqVy_4xb
An RCT found that adding a mobile app to traditional CBT was more effective than CBT alone for key clinical outcomes in college students.
Why it matters:
Blended care approaches can extend the reach and effectiveness of therapy, especially for young adults.

📍 3. AI should be transparent and support, not replace, human connection
🔗 https://lnkd.in/gUfZ997n\xa0
Young people find AI-driven resource recommendations acceptable only when it’s clear the interaction isn’t human, preserving the value of real peer and clinician support.
Why it matters:
Use AI thoughtfully, as a tool for signposting and support, with clear communication about data use and without mimicking empathy.

📍 4. Social media recruitment can be faster, cheaper, and more inclusive
🔗 https://lnkd.in/gcZ6e6sU
Recruiting participants via social media was quicker, more cost-effective, and reached a more diverse group, including higher-risk individuals, compared to traditional methods.
Why it matters:
Leveraging targeted video ads, detailed landing pages, and pixel tracking can help researchers and programs reach underserved and vulnerable populations more effectively.

If you want to discuss these findings or digital mental health trends, feel free to connect.
#DigitalHealth #MentalHealthResearch #YouthMentalHealth #CBT #BlendedCare']

Name: 1_planning_review_interventions_severe
Representative_Docs:
['This is a big moment for England’s mental health. For many years, we’ve been calling for a radical shift towards a cross-government approach with a focus on prevention, and we look forward to contributing to the plan’s success.  

Our nation’s mental health has gone downhill over recent decades due to things like the Covid-19 pandemic and the legacy of austerity. But change is possible, and we are pleased that the government is taking action.   

We will be advocating for the plan to be ambitious, evidence-led and with a real focus on those communities most at risk of poor mental health; and to address the social and economic factors that shape all our mental health.', '🚀 Our new umbrella review (i.e., a review of reviews) led by Lasse Brandt and Jurjen Luykx on climate-related and nature-based interventions for mental health is out in JAMA Psychiatry

🔎 Despite growing evidence on the impacts of climate change on mental health, we still know little about what evidence-based interventions should be implemented to address these mental health outcomes

💡 This was largely confirmed by the current umbrella review which found limited evidence for mental health interventions to reduce adverse mental health impacts of climatic impact drivers

🌲 I was particularly surprised to see that the review failed to identify systematic reviews and/or meta-analyses of nature-based interventions aimed at reducing the adverse mental health impacts of natural environment loss, increased temperatures, air pollution, and other climatic impact drivers

🌱 While the authors highlights that there is promising potential for future research in this field based on evidence from contexts other than climate change, this paper is another call to action concerning the need for more evidence on mental health interventions for contexts affected by climate change

#mentalhealth #climatechange #interventions

https://lnkd.in/eb5PEpYP', "Today, we're announcing a partnership that represents an important step towards transforming lives for millions: CHAI is joining forces with Wellcome Trust and MedAccess to help people with severe mental health conditions access the treatment they need.
 
The reality is stark: Roughly one billion people suffer from mental health conditions, yet more than 70% of people living with psychosis and severe conditions in low- and middle-income countries receive no care at all. Not because treatments don't exist—but because they are unavailable or unaffordable.
 
Millions of people living with schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, severe depression, or debilitating anxiety lack access to medication that could help them lead a fuller life. 
 
Through this 12-month program in Ethiopia, Nigeria, Rwanda, and South Africa, we're working to change that by:
✅Making proven medications more accessible and affordable
✅Working with governments to prioritize cost-effective mental health treatments
✅Bringing manufacturers and policymakers together to introduce newer, effective treatments faster
 
Our vision is a future where everyone with a severe mental condition can reliably access quality treatment—regardless of where they live or their economic circumstances. 🌍
 
This #WorldMentalHealthDay, we're committed to making universal access to mental health treatment a reality, not just an aspiration.
 
Because mental health is health. And everyone deserves care.
 
#MentalHealth #GlobalHealth #HealthEquity
Neil Buddy Shah Professor Miranda Wolpert Michael Anderson CB"]

Name: 2_early_young_youth_children
Representative_Docs:
['"There is no health without mental health" - Dr Sy Saeed said it well during our panel today with Dr. Gary Maslow at the NC Chamber Mental Health Summit. They made a compelling case for business leaders to invest in youth mental health to support our future workforce and address rising healthcare costs. 

I’m proud that the Blue Cross and Blue Shield of North Carolina Foundation is investing $1.5M to expand Dr Saeed's NC Statewide Telepsychiatry Program (NC‑STeP) to the University of North Carolina at Pembroke. 

When most mental health conditions begin by age 24, we know the importance of getting upstream - investing in prevention and bringing care to places students already know and trust. Delivering services directly on campus helps reduce barriers and ensures support is there when it’s needed.

Grateful for our partners at East Carolina University, NC‑STeP, and UNC Pembroke, and for the shared commitment to improving youth mental health across North Carolinia. #NCMentalHealthSummit26', "I’m so proud to see this level of trust and endorsement for Soluna - huge thanks to our team that have been so crucial to ensuring that Soluna is delivering effective and safe care to the youth of California that need our support. 

It's this kind of alignment - where our services are rooted in the real-world networks of trusted professionals that know their communities best - that has always been so crucial to our model at Kooth. 

25 years of delivering digital mental health services have shown us that building a great service is not enough. Digital services need to be trusted and rooted in the communities they serve. Building and commissioning an amazing product or service is useless if nobody uses it. 

As with the UK, it's clear that when support is available at no cost, requires no referral (or insurance), and is designed to complement the work already taking place in schools, it can become part of the fabric of how we care for young people.

I’m grateful to our partners across California for their leadership and for continuing to move this work forward. This is the kind of progress that expands access, strengthens systems, and ultimately improves outcomes for young people.", 'Children and Young People’s Mental Health Week is a powerful reminder that supporting emotional wellbeing must begin early. We know that most mental health conditions begin before the age of 25 and prevalence is increasing, which is why a life course approach, starting with strong foundations in early childhood and adolescence, is central to our mission.

We are investing a further £7 million for 24 Early Support Hubs across England offering flexible, drop-in support for 11-25-year-olds. Whether its counselling, psychological therapies, or wider advice on sexual health, education, employment, substances and financial concerns, these services are about ensuring young people can access help early, without pressure or stigma.

Through the\xa010 Year Health Plan, I’m proud to say we are also continuing to expand Mental Health Support Teams in schools and colleges and expect to reach full national coverage by 2029. These teams provide early, practical support where young people already spend their day, helping them manage worries before they become overwhelming.

But early support is not just about services. Parents, carers, teachers and peers play some of the most important roles in a young person’s mental health, often long before clinical professionals become involved. When children and young people feel heard, understood and supported, at home and in their communities, difficult emotions become easier to manage and seeking help becomes far less daunting. Guidance from\xa0Every Mind Matters shows one of the simplest things we can all do to support children and young people is to listen and connect.

This week, as schools, families and communities come together to raise awareness, the message is clear: we all have a role to play supporting children and young people’s mental health and helping every child and young person feel safe and that they belong.

https://lnkd.in/erATQ4W7']

Name: 3_month_environments_reminder_supported
Representative_Docs:
['Mental Health Month is a reminder that performance is not only driven by skills and targets — it is driven by people.
In today’s workplace, mental health is not a “nice to have.” It is a business priority. Teams that feel safe, supported, and heard are the ones that sustain performance, innovation, and resilience.
As leaders, our role is clear:
Create environments where people can speak, recover, and perform at their best.
Because behind every mission , there is a human being.
Manpower Egypt', 'Mental Health Awareness Month is an important reminder that well-being has to be part of how we think about the way people work and grow.

People bring their full lives with them into the workplace. Stress, caregiving, burnout, grief and uncertainty do not stay neatly outside the office door.

That is why supportive cultures matter. When people feel safe enough to ask for help and supported enough to say what they need, they are better able to show up fully in their work. Mental health is personal, but supporting it is something we all have a role in. #MentalHealthAwarenessMonth #Wellbeing', 'Mental Health Awareness Month in the U.S. and Mental Health Awareness Week in the U.K. are important reminders that supporting well-being must be an ongoing commitment.
\xa0
Creating environments where people feel supported, connected, and empowered to prioritize their well-being helps individuals and teams thrive. From emotional and social well-being to career and financial support, meaningful resources and open conversations can make a lasting impact.
\xa0
This month and beyond, let’s continue encouraging conversations around mental health and reminding one another that seeking support is a sign of strength.
#MentalHealthAwarenessMonth #MentalHealthAwarenessWeek']

Name: 4_kids_activities_hosted_events
Representative_Docs:
['🧠Last week was Mental Health Awareness Week. To mark this, we hosted a range of activities designed to support wellbeing, encourage open conversations, and bring colleagues together.

🌱On Tuesday, we had Jen Davis from Flourish Nutrition come in to speak about the benefits that good nutrition can have on your cognitive function and mental health. The theme of MHAW this year is take action, so we wanted to give colleagues actionable insights into how best to support their mental health and wellbeing.

🍰On Wednesday, things got competitive when we hosted the Great Dockyard Bake Off. Bakers competed for the crown of Best Baker on Base and they did not disappoint! Congrats to Billy Bulloch‘s daughter Scar for claiming victory with an excellent Sandy Surprise Cake! We are proud to say that the proceeds of selling the bakes raised over £500 for Solent Mind.

🥾 Thursday saw KBS colleagues go on a mental health walk with Solent Original Walks around Old Portsmouth. It was a great opportunity to boost our knowledge of Portsmouth’s history, uncover hidden gems and hear underrated tales of Portsmouth’s past with our brilliant guide John whilst encouraging important conversations about mental health.

🌟Thank you to everyone who’s help and support helped make this week happen!', 'Every child deserves to feel seen, heard and supported. Children’s Mental Health Acceptance Day is a reminder that mental health is part of overall health, and that families, caregivers and communities all play a role in creating spaces where young people can thrive.

This May, Oregon Family Support Network will host two gatherings to bring that spirit of acceptance to life. Each gathering is shaped by input from youth and families and offers opportunities to connect, participate in activities and access resources that support families. 

For many families, the journey through mental and behavioral health systems can feel overwhelming. Moments of connection, like these events, create space for relief, understanding and hope.

They remind families:
•	You are not alone
•	Your voice matters
•	Support is available

To learn more about the events, visit Oregon Family Support Network’s event page: https://ow.ly/9rvB50YVQnZ', "Last weekend, we kicked off Mental Health Month with a powerful reminder of why this work matters so deeply. Nationwide Children's Hospital proudly founded The Kids Mental Health Foundation and our teams were honored to join communities in Columbus and Los Angeles for a series of events elevating one message: children’s mental health starts with each of us.
\xa0
In Columbus, we celebrated Columbus Crew Title Night, where our remarkable patient champions proudly delivered the ceremonial match ball.
\xa0
In Los Angeles, a thought‑provoking panel hosted by Hollywood and Mind to demonstrate how storytelling can change the national conversation around kids’ mental health. We also gathered for an event to honor champions whose advocacy is driving meaningful progress for children and families. Their leadership inspires all of us.
\xa0
And at Kids Mental Health Day at The Grove, families experienced a full day of activities designed to show that mental health is a vital part of every child’s upbringing.
\xa0
As we move through Mental Health Month, I’m grateful for every partner, advocate and family who stands with us. Together, we are building a future where kids’ mental health is understood, supported and prioritized."]

Name: 5_city_office_law_director
Representative_Docs:
["New York City Mayor Eric Adams and NYC Mayor's Office of Community Mental Health’s (OCMH) Executive Director Eva Wong released the city’s first-ever\xa0Involuntary Transports Dashboard, offering the public unprecedented access to data on involuntary transports for hospital evaluation of individuals in mental health crisis.
\xa0
“This launch reflects our unwavering commitment to ensure those in crisis receive the care they deserve while keeping public spaces safe,” said\xa0OCMH Executive Director Eva\xa0Wong. “It underscores the City’s focus on transparency, accountability, and coordinated response to urgent needs.”

The dashboard affirms NYC’s dual commitment to compassionate care and public safety — both essential to community well-being.

🔗 View the dashboard: https://lnkd.in/eChVg9V8 

📌 Read the press release: https://lnkd.in/e8pdGkBY

🗞️ Read coverage via MSN/Bloomberg: https://lnkd.in/e6WGbhgc

#NYC #MentalHealth #NYCMentalHealth #InvoluntaryTransport #CompassionateCare", 'Mental Health Awareness Month in May

The Frederick County Mental Health Court Team links its participants, people charged with misdemeanor offenses, to critical resources resulting in their decreased recidivism, improved quality of life and reduced reliance on psychiatric hospitalization.  
The Team includes the Presiding Judge, Frederick County State’s Attorney’s Office, MD Office of the Public Defender, Division of Parole and Probation, Mental Health Court Coordinator, Frederick Police Department  and the Frederick County Health Department.  
We thank the wonderful mental health community organizations for providing their services and we celebrated our most recent graduating class on March 11, 2026! 
Chief of the District Court, Assistant State’s Attorney, Kristin King, is committed to accommodating the voluminous yet uniquely specialized docket for these cases.  Referrals to the program can be accepted from defendants themselves, community organizations, law enforcement, attorneys, family members and more!
The District Court of Maryland for Frederick County’s Mental Health Court is a specialized intervention program designed for individuals diagnosed with a mental health condition and charged with misdemeanor offenses. This voluntary program aims to connect participants with appropriate treatment and support services within the community, with the goal of enhancing overall quality of life.
The Mental Health Court embodies a collaborative approach among justice system professionals committed to deepening their understanding of mental health issues. Participants are assigned to a specialized docket overseen by the Mental Health Court team, which delivers a non-adversarial, individualized process tailored to meet each participant’s unique needs.
For more information regarding this program, please contact the Mental Health Court Coordinator at 301-600-2656.
https://lnkd.in/epPZEwwm
https://lnkd.in/eYFDYQ-R', 'The Department of Mental Health (DMH) is dedicated to improving mental health for all individuals living in Massachusetts. At its core, DMH is an agency that wants to help people, and that is committed to promoting positive mental health and improving access to mental health care across the Commonwealth. DMH is particularly focused on equity and reaching communities that have historically been underserved.

DMH serves individuals and families across the state and has a specialized role in the healthcare delivery system. DMH supports promotion and prevention efforts and provides low barrier services available to everyone across Massachusetts. The Office of Behavioral Health Promotion & Prevention promotes wellness among all residents of the Commonwealth through education, outreach, and supporting philosophically aligned programs and initiatives across the state.

DMH also connects people to appropriate wellness services, including community resources. This is done through programs such as Young Adult Access Centers and Recovery Learning Communities which are free and available to everyone in Massachusetts. Additionally, DMH engages in awareness and education efforts, community outreach, early intervention, peer supports, mental health policy and regulation, licensure of private acute mental health facilities, as well as directly providing additional services, treatment, and supports.

For individuals with more serious needs, the Department provides a variety community-based and inpatient services which are not available elsewhere.

The Behavioral Health Help Line, which launched in 2023, connects people with services whether or not they have insurance, and whether they are seeking help for themselves or a loved one. Residents can call or text 833-773-2445 any time of the day or night, and in 200 languages. An option to chat with someone is also available by visiting www.masshelpline.com.']

Name: 6_employees_workplace_report_financial
Representative_Docs:
['The good news is that about 54% of employees (2024) feel their employer cares about their mental health (up 6% from 2023). The bad news is that almost 40% of employees report high stress at work. This translates into an added $5.3 million in cost annually for every 1,000 employees, which is pretty staggering. These costs hit 3 main areas: cost control (higher health claims), risk mitigation (making mistakes, missing deadlines, cutting corners), and sustained performance (increased sick days, lower engagement).\xa0Are we asking the right questions of our people? Questions such as:” How often do you feel stressed, anxious, or overwhelmed at work?” Followed by: “To what extent do your workload and deadlines feel manageable?” And” Do you feel psychologically safe raising concerns or asking for help?” I think the best line in this article is “Resilience isn’t a program- it is a system embedded into how organizations lead, operate and grow.” \xa0Perhaps the most important question of all is one we ask ourselves as leaders: “How are we doing?”', 'These statistics are not merely numbers—they are signals of a workplace mental health crisis.

In the past 30 days alone:
• 51% of employees reported crying due to work stress.
• 52% experienced anxiety or panic-like symptoms at work.
• 65% felt compelled to hide their struggles to avoid appearing weak.
The last statistic concerns me the most.

When employees believe they must suffer in silence, something is fundamentally wrong with the culture. Whether in academia, healthcare, government, or the corporate sector, people should not have to choose between protecting their well-being and protecting their professional image.

Behind every percentage is a person—a colleague, faculty member, manager, healthcare worker, researcher, parent, or friend—trying to carry invisible burdens while continuing to perform, contribute, and persevere.

Mental health is not a personal weakness. It is a workplace issue, a leadership issue, and increasingly, an organizational responsibility. Perhaps the question we should be asking is not, “How resilient are our employees?”
Instead: “What are we doing that requires so much resilience in the first place?”

Source: Modern Health Workforce Mental Health Report (April 2026)
https://lnkd.in/e3tJixVk', 'May is mental health month. Thanks to two of our Cleveland Clinic Canada corporate advisory partners Susy Michor from TD Bank and Karen Smith, MBA from Bruce Power for their open discussion on mental health in the workplace at the HRMI conference in Muskoka. 

A few key points on this important topic:
- ~47% of employees reported experiencing burnout symptoms in 2025
- ~70% of Canadian employees say their productivity was negatively impacted by their mental health in 2025
-  The financial impact on Canadian Employers is measured in billions annually, and mental health disorders have become the leading cause of long-term disability claims.

Many organizations have made substantial investments in mental health, yet our outcomes have not improved. 

Ultimately employees need to feel safe in asking for help, and leaders can help create that safety through their visible support. 

And not only is early mental health support important, but so is prevention. We can improve an employee’s mental health by simply making the workplace a great place to work. We know this is often achieved by preventing burnout and focusing on:
 - creating community & connection in the workplace
- allowing for control & autonomy in one’s work
- working on meaningful work
- through reward & recognition
- fairness & flexibility in the workplace

When benefits match a strong education and culture, that’s when we truly begin to move the needle.']

Name: 7_board_safety_settings_carers
Representative_Docs:
['The Mental Health Commission has launched new guidance aimed at strengthening the quality, safety and accessibility of mental health services for adults and children with intellectual disabilities. 

The purpose of this guidance is to support staff in delivering care and treatment to individuals with co-occurring mental ill-health and intellectual disabilities across all mental health service settings for adults and children, including inpatient units, community residences and community-based services. 

It promotes a person-centred approach that upholds and protects human rights, ensuring that care is inclusive, responsive and respectful of individual needs. In doing so, it supports services to continually build their capability, enhance professional confidence, and embed practices that lead to better outcomes for individuals and families.

Read more at https://ow.ly/lSjf50YYVnX.

Pictured is Minister for Mental Health, Mary Butler, and the Chief Executive of the Mental Health Commission, John Farrelly, with the new guidance.', 'Really pleased to share that our paper, “Investigating the protective effects of social support on forensic inpatient admissions”, has now been published 🎉
This project, led by Dr Andy Charalambous alongside Gerald (Jerry) Burgess and Rachel Holden, explores a question that often feels intuitively true but is surprisingly under-researched: how much do supportive relationships actually protect people within forensic mental health settings?

Using NHS clinical records and structured risk assessments (HCR-20v3), we analysed data from 330 service users to look at outcomes such as length of stay, incidents, and readmissions. What we found adds weight to something many clinicians will recognise—family and close relationships can play a meaningful protective role, particularly in reducing violence and repeat admissions.

For me, this was a great opportunity to work deeply with complex clinical datasets and apply statistical modelling to real-world service questions.
I’m really grateful to have been part of this collaboration and to my co-authors for their insight and rigour throughout.

The paper is available here: https://lnkd.in/e4Rmskza
Would be very interested to hear thoughts around how we can better integrate family and social support into care.', 'Strong governance supports strong outcomes for young people.

At Parkville Youth Mental Health and Wellbeing Service, our board plays a critical role in shaping safe, effective and accountable care.

Our nine independent non‑executive directors bring deep expertise across youth mental health, governance and service delivery. Importantly, our board includes people with lived experience of mental health challenges, alongside family members and carers.

These perspectives strengthen decision‑making and keep our strategy grounded in the real experiences of navigating the mental health system.

Together, our board provides clear oversight and values‑led leadership. Their guidance supports our delivery of evidence‑based, trauma‑informed care and drives continuous improvement across the service system - always centred on the needs of young people.

Meet our board 👉  https://lnkd.in/gYbVA7Ag

#HereForYouth #PYMHWS']

Name: 8_student_counseling_california_higher
Representative_Docs:
["Mental health is health. 💙 

This Mental Health Awareness Month, we're proud to shine a spotlight on the vital role mental health plays in overall health and well-being — and to celebrate our dedicated mental health care physicians and professionals who deliver high-quality, evidence-based care to our patients every day.

Their work is making a difference: For seven consecutive years, Kaiser Permanente Northern California has been designated a best-rated health plan for behavioral and mental health care by California's Center for Data Insights and Innovation. This also marks the seventh year in a row that our California plans have been the only 5-out-of-5-star ranking recipients in the state. ⭐ 

Behind that recognition is real investment and growth: Kaiser Permanente has invested more than $2 billion since 2020 to expand mental health facilities and train and license new clinicians across the organization, and increased our mental health workforce in Northern California by 25%. And through our Kaiser Permanente Northern California Mental Health Training Collaborative, the largest non-governmental mental health training program in the U.S., we're training 600 aspiring therapists and psychologists this year alone.

To all our mental health care providers: Thank you for showing up for patients, families, and members across Northern California. Your commitment to compassionate whole-person care is at the heart of what we do. 🙏

🔗 Learn about our mental health and wellness resources: 
https://lnkd.in/esh8rVbz
\xa0
#MentalHealthAwarenessMonth", 'Are you hoping to make a difference in the the landscape of mental health and higher education?!

Ryan Travia, Ed.D., FACHA, Marcus Hotaling and I are excited to share the call for proposals for a new NASPA publication, "Transforming Mental Health in Higher Education: Leadership Strategies and Innovative Approaches".

We invite chapter proposals for a forthcoming edited book examining leadership strategies and innovative approaches to mental health service delivery in higher education, with a particular focus on post-pandemic transformations in counseling center operations, institutional mental health frameworks, and community-based approaches to student well-being.

This comprehensive volume will analyze contemporary challenges including mental health policy implications, evidence-based treatment modalities, crisis intervention protocols, resource allocation strategies, and the pedagogical integration of faculty as mental health allies within institutional ecosystems.

We seek empirically grounded contributions from counseling center directors, student affairs practitioners, faculty scholars, and clinical researchers who can provide theoretical frameworks, methodological innovations, and applied research findings that advance our understanding of contemporary collegiate mental health service delivery. The book will synthesize scholarship with practitioner wisdom through various sections, while examining institutional variability across sectors (public, private, community colleges, etc.). 

We are committed to centering diverse scholarly voices and identities to ensure representation in this essential resource for mental health research, policy, and practice in higher education. Please note that authors of accepted proposals may be paired with a co-author(s) to ensure the broadest representation possible and are subject to final approval by NASPA. For more information about the chapters and specific details, please see the following: https://lnkd.in/e2MrBnjA. Note that if you are not a current NASPA member, you will be prompted to create a free account.', 'If you care about student mental health—and especially outcomes in community college work—this research is a must-read.

A new CCMH study by Scofield & Abelson takes a deeper look at how counseling centers differ between two-year/community colleges and four-year institutions—and the findings should prompt serious reflection across our field. 

Here’s what stood out to me:
🔹 We’re serving fewer students—but with equal (or greater) strain: 
Community colleges are reaching a smaller share of their student body with counseling services, even while clinicians carry slightly higher caseloads.
🔹 Access, not demand, may be the limiting factor
We know community college students experience significant mental health challenges—often compounded by financial stress and barriers to care. 
🔹 Different models are filling the gaps: Compared to four-year institutions, community college centers are more likely to:
Use session limits and less frequent appointments
Lean into peer support, coaching, and screening tools
Serve in co-responder roles alongside campus safety
🔹 Infrastructure gaps persist
Less access to professional affiliations, case management, and clinical vendor supports raises important questions about long-term sustainability and student outcomes.

So what do we do with this?
For me, this study reinforces a simple but urgent reality:
👉 We cannot achieve equitable student success without rethinking how mental health systems are designed, resourced, and delivered in community colleges.

A call to action for leaders, practitioners, and partners:
➡️ Pause and assess: Are we truly meeting the mental health needs of our students—or just those who can access services as currently structured?
➡️ Re-examine models: Is our “dose” of care aligned with outcomes we expect (retention, persistence, completion)?
➡️ Invest intentionally: Are we resourcing counseling, case management, and upstream supports at levels that reflect the realities of our student population?
➡️ Engage collectively: How can we partner—across institutions, systems, and organizations—to close these gaps?

Community colleges educate nearly half of all undergraduates—and often those with the greatest barriers.

We have an opportunity—and a responsibility—to build mental health systems that truly meet that moment.

💬 I’d welcome perspectives from others in the field—what are you seeing on your campus, and what’s shifting in your model right now?
#StudentSuccess #CommunityColleges #MentalHealthMatters #HigherEducationLeadership #EquityInEducation #CCMH #StudentWellbeing']

Name: 9_988_disorder_24_division
Representative_Docs:
['May is Mental Health Awareness Month.

I’ve been pretty open over the years about my own experience: it’s real, it’s common, and it deserves the same attention we give anything else in our life that impacts our ability to live rich and rewarding lives.

There’s a version of our jobs, and frankly, a version of this world, that rewards pushing through, ignoring the signals in your gut, and telling yourself you’ll deal with it later. I’ve done that. It doesn’t work for long. 

So my message is simple: Talk openly about mental health. Normalize it. Take it seriously. Make time for your mental health in the same way you would any other health problem.\xa0 

Building things that last means making sure you’re around and healthy to see that vision come to life.', "Mental Health Awareness Week starts today, and this year's theme, 'Action', is a direct invitation to do something, not just mark the occasion.

One of the most practical things you can do this week is take free online suicide awareness training. On Tuesday, 12 May (1–2.30 pm), thriveldn and zero-suicide-alliance are running a 90-minute session covering how to recognise warning signs, start a direct conversation, use appropriate language, and signpost someone to professional support.

The training is funded by the MayorofLondon and open to anyone — whether you work in the NHS, a local authority, a community organisation, or simply want to feel more prepared to support people in your own life. No prior experience or specialist knowledge is needed.

When thriveldn ran the same session in January, 98% of the 400 people who gave feedback said they'd recommend it to a friend or colleague. It received an average quality rating of 4.7 out of 5.

Register via Eventbrite: https://tphc.info/4dfom3h

Throughout the week, we'll also be sharing content on physical activity and mental health, adult ADHD pathways, and our recent work in North Central London.

#MentalHealthAwarenessWeek #MHAW2026", 'May is Mental Health Awareness Month. During this time, it’s important to remember that mental health is health.

The stigma surrounding mental health often keeps people from reaching out, but there is no shame in seeking support.  If you or someone you know is struggling with mental or behavioral health, it is important to get assistance or treatment. 

The Suffolk County Department of Health Services has a dedicated staff in the Division of Community Mental Hygiene Services whose role is to assist people with mental illness and substance use disorders connect to services and treatment options in Suffolk County.

There are many ways a person can reach out for support including:
• Call or text 988 to chat with a member of the lifeline 24/7 who can provide support or services.
• If you need a safe place to go in person, visit FSL-DASH, located at 90 Adams Ave. in Hauppauge or call 631-650-0110.
• Contact the FSL Mobile Crisis Team at 631-952-3333 who can provide support over the phone or come to you.
• Access the Suffolk County Directory of Behavioral Health Services website 24/7 to find services that include prevention, harm reduction, treatment, recovery and support.
• For non-urgent assistance, contact the 311 Call Center by dialing 311 to connect with someone who can direct you to the appropriate services.
• For hospital-based emergency psychiatric services, call Stony Brook Medicine CPEP at 631-444-2990
• If someone is a danger to themselves or someone else, call 911.

For more information on the Suffolk County Division of Community Mental Hygiene Services, visit https://lnkd.in/ebYBNHFz or call 631-853-8500.']

Name: 10_insurance_women_sustained_safe
Representative_Docs:
['Maternal mental health is not a side issue in maternal health. It is a driver of outcomes.

In our Safe Babies Safe Moms work, we saw clearly that you cannot improve maternal and infant outcomes without directly addressing perinatal mental health.

Yet too often, systems are designed to screen without the infrastructure to treat.

This is why we built an integrated perinatal mental health model within Safe Babies Safe Moms that does more than identify need. It responds to it.

Our approach connects screening, clinical care, behavioral health, and social support into a coordinated system across pregnancy, birth, and postpartum.

Because identifying depression, anxiety, or trauma is only the first step.
What matters is what the system does next.

In maternal health, gaps in follow-up are not just inefficiencies. They are risks.
When mental health care is embedded into the structure of care delivery, not treated as an add-on, we begin to see what is possible.

Better engagement
Earlier intervention
Improved outcomes for both mother and baby

Maternal mental health is not separate from maternal safety. It is central to it.

That is the work we are continuing to build through Safe Babies Safe Moms.

#maternalhealth #mentalhealth #safebabiessafemoms', 'Maternal mental health outcomes improve when care is standardized, not improvised.

In Safe Babies Safe Moms, we built a perinatal mental health care continuum that removes ambiguity from clinical decision-making and replaces it with structured, evidence-informed pathways.

Because in maternal health, inconsistency creates risk.

Following screening, patients are not left with a vague referral or a wait-and-see approach.

They enter a defined decision tree.

Mild symptoms are addressed with early, targeted interventions.
Moderate symptoms trigger psychotherapy and coordinated care planning.
Severe symptoms activate higher-intensity services, including psychiatry and medication management.
Critical presentations prompt immediate safety assessment and escalation.

At every level, the response is intentional.
Care is matched to need.
Teams are aligned.

Patients are monitored over time, not just at a single point.

This is what it means to operationalize maternal mental health.

Not just identifying symptoms, but building a system that knows exactly what to do next.

Because when pathways are unclear, patients fall through the cracks.
When pathways are defined, outcomes improve.

And in maternal health, that difference matters.', 'What happens when insurance-based mental health care is built into Ob/Gyn care?\xa0
⠀
In our new study, we examined access to treatment for Women’s Mental Health @Ob/Gyn, an insurance-based mental health service embedded within Ob/Gyn practices.\xa0
⠀
Analyzing nearly three years of data from more than 2,600 women, we asked: Does integration combined with accepting insurance improve access and sustained engagement?\xa0
⠀
Key findings:\xa0
⠀
• 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐫 𝐚𝐜𝐜𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 𝐞𝐱𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐞𝐝: 65% of referred women initiated care—well above rates reported in comparable programs.\xa0
⠀
• 𝐌𝐞𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠𝐟𝐮𝐥 𝐬𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭: Over one-third attended three or more visits, with the highest likelihood of sustained engagement among pregnant, postpartum, and women who had experienced miscarriage or stillbirth.\xa0
⠀
• 𝐁𝐫𝐨𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐜𝐡: Nearly 40% of those who initiated care were neither pregnant nor trying to conceive, underscoring unmet mental health needs across the Ob/Gyn lifespan.\xa0
⠀
• 𝐏𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐚𝐥 𝐛𝐚𝐫𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐫𝐬: Gaps in insurance coverage—particularly plans that cover physical but outsource for mental health care—significantly limited sustained engagement.\xa0
⠀
The takeaway is clear: embedding insurance-based mental health into Ob/Gyn care is both feasible and impactful. At the same time, barriers in coverage continue to constrain the full potential of behavioral health integration.\xa0
⠀
I’m grateful to our clinical teams, collaborators, and funders who made this work possible. We hope these findings contribute to policy and systems changes that honor the WHO’s adage: there is no health without mental health.\xa0
⠀
🔗 Full article: https://lnkd.in/ejddKthS']

#WorldMentalHealthDay", 'The Mental Health Innovation Network (MHIN) Asia Hub, together with the ISC Mental Health and Psychosocial Support (MHPSS) Learning Collaborative at the Center for Innovation in Mental Health (CIMH), are pleased to host this webinar on the implementation of the WHO Comprehensive Mental Health Action Plan 2013–2030 in the Western Pacific (WPRO) and South-East Asia (SEARO) regions.

The session will feature regional and country-level perspectives, including contributions from WHO SEARO and WHO WPRO, highlighting progress, innovations, challenges, and emerging opportunities across diverse contexts. Speakers will share practical examples of implementation strategies, tools, and mechanisms that have strengthened mental health systems, and will offer insights to foster an open and interactive dialogue.

The webinar aims to create a dynamic space for regional exchange and peer learning, and will examine how partnerships - including networks and coalitions such as the MHPSS Learning Collaborative and MHIN - can accelerate implementation and support more coordinated and effective mental health policy and service development.', "As the community mental health sector continues to grow and evolve, traineeships offer a practical pathway to build workforce capability and support future workforce needs. 

Join our upcoming Workforce development in practice webinar, where we will explore peer work training pathways in NSW, the benefits and requirements of traineeships, and the practical supports available to organisations, supervisors and learners.

This webinar is open to the broader community mental health sector, providing an opportunity for both MHCC members and non-members to learn more about workforce development pathways and available supports.  

When: Monday 15 June, 10:00am to 11:15am 
Where: Online via Zoom

Hosted by our CEO Dr Evelyne Tadros, we'll hear from our speakers, Michelle Hoomans , Learning and Development Manager, Mental Health Coordinating Council, Jessica Radican (English), Principal Statewide Peer (Lived Experience) Workforce Officer, Mental Health Branch, NSW Ministry of Health, Susan Scowcroft, Executive Director, NSW Community Services and Health Industry Training Advisory Body, and Leon Esho, Training Advisor, Training Services, NSW Department of Education. 

Register now: https://lnkd.in/genq7Bgd"]

Name: 12_right_government_pathways_options
Representative_Docs:
["Mental health is not a luxury, it is a necessity, especially for those navigating vulnerability, uncertainty, and systemic neglect.
On May 20, Tdh is co-hosting an event dedicated to mental health, bringing together voices and perspectives that are too often overlooked. This discussion is part of a broader series of events we are organizing in the coming months to shed light on the very real consequences of political decisions, particularly funding cuts and the deprioritization of critical issues. Behind every budget line are lives impacted. Vulnerable groups are often the first to feel these shifts, yet their needs remain the least visible in policy debates. Through this event, we aim to create space for dialogue, reflection, and, importantly, action. Raising awareness is only the first step, what follows must be a renewed commitment to protecting and prioritizing mental health for all. Join us on May 20! Terre des hommes foundation, Gates Foundation Children's Investment Fund Foundation (CIFF) Grand Challenges Canada", "We believe better mental health support is possible. That's why we've joined 20+ mental health charities to launch the Head On - a campaign to turn public consensus on mental health into political action. We want to go beyond changing conversations and change lives.

Together, we can build a modern mental health system. One where everyone gets the right support, at the right time. This starts by giving mental health the political focus it deserves. 

“Head On brings together over 20 charities who are backed by public opinion in demanding mental health gets the political attention it deserves. We must fund and scale what works – with a focus on driving down mental health waiting lists and prioritising prevention, early intervention and community care. Action can’t wait, and we urgently need a clear, ambitious, strategic vision for our nation’s mental health from the Government.” 
– Andy Bell

Find out more at https://headoncampaign.org.

#HeadOnCampaign", 'Yesterday, Coast Mental Health CEO Keir Macdonald appeared before the Select Standing Committee on Finance and Government Services.

The message is clear, it is not about asking government to spend more. It is about delivering better outcomes for people and better value for taxpayers by investing differently.

Across BC, people are cycling between homelessness, hospitals, emergency departments, shelters and correctional facilities. This cycle is costly, ineffective and preventable, and reflects persistent gaps in housing, supports and pathways to stability.

We urge government to take a more strategic approach and focus investments where they can have the greatest impact by advancing three key priorities:

1. Invest in the right housing with the right supports
People need access to housing that matches their needs at the right time. Today, too many are forced into options that do not support their recovery because key parts of the continuum are missing or the right supports are unavailable. We see people completing treatment with nowhere appropriate to go, others remaining in higher levels of care because there are no alternatives, and many cycling back into crisis as a result.

2. Invest in pathways to employment, education and community participation
Housing and health care are essential, but they are not enough. People also need opportunities to build meaningful, connected lives. Today, only 10 to 15 per cent of people living with severe mental illness are employed, despite many wanting and being able to work with the right supports. Better aligning existing investments can help create real pathways to employment, education and community inclusion.

3. Strengthen pathways from custody back to community
Correctional facilities are increasingly managing challenges they were never designed to address. One of the biggest barriers people face upon release is not access to health care, it is access to housing. While Community Transition Teams play a critical role, there is an opportunity to strengthen their impact by embedding housing expertise. This would help ensure people leave custody with a pathway to stability, not a return to homelessness.

The path forward is clear.

By shifting where and how we invest, we can reduce crisis, improve outcomes and build a more effective and compassionate system for people living with mental illness in British Columbia.']

Name: 13_practice_trained_medicine_performance
Representative_Docs:
["I’m pleased to share a new publication in the Journal of Sport and Social Issues:
Mental Health Marketing through Sport: A Point–Counterpoint Discussion. In this dialogue paper, Beth Cianfrone and I bring together sport marketing and sport sociology perspectives to examine the growing presence of mental health sponsorships and endorsements in sport. While these partnerships are often framed as stigma-reducing and socially responsible, they also raise important ethical and theoretical questions related to branding, neoliberalism, and the commodification of mental health care.

We ask questions like: 
-Do athlete endorsements and organizational sponsorships of mental health companies reduce stigma or repackage it?
-What happens when care becomes a branded, for-profit product?
-Who actually benefits from these partnerships?

This paper is meant to spark conversation across disciplines and between scholars, practitioners, and sport leaders. We’d love for you to read it, and we'd love to hear your thoughts. 

https://lnkd.in/en5wkn5u", '🚨 New esports mental health editorial 🚨

"Time to Monitor the Mental Health of Esports Athletes" is now published in the British Journal of Sports Medicine (BJSM).

Over the past few years, esports has attracted a level of institutional recognition that would have seemed unlikely a decade ago, from the International Olympic Committee – IOC's Olympic Esports Series and Esports Week to the Esports World Cup and Esports Nations Cup (Esports Foundation), which have drawn millions of viewers globally. The competitive infrastructure around esports players is growing fast.

But institutional recognition without a corresponding duty of care is an incomplete step forward. Here, we argue that stakeholders in sports medicine, public health, and esports must work together to extend structured mental health monitoring to those competing on digital fields.

💡 The core argument:

Esports athletes train and compete under conditions that closely mirror those of traditional sports, including high-performance pressure, public scrutiny, short contracts, and blurred boundaries between training and recovery. Anxiety symptoms have been reported in 38–82% of players, and depressive symptoms in 25–37% in some samples. Yet unlike athletes in traditional sports, most esports players have no formal mental health surveillance system watching over them.

The sports medicine community has already built tools and frameworks, such as the IOC consensus statement (Margo Mountjoy, Scott Sloan, Vincent Gouttebarge) on mental health and the Sports Mental Health Assessment Tool (SMHAT), that could, with adaptation, be extended to esports. Early initiatives from Riot Games, BLAST, The GG Project, and The FUSE Cup - International School Esports show integration is feasible. Alongside these, organisations such as Movember have been investing directly in esports-specific research and programs.

What's missing is coordinated leadership to make it standard practice.

Mental health monitoring is not an optional enhancement. It is a foundational component of athlete care, and esports athletes deserve the same standard.

Huge thanks to my co-author, Simon Rice. 

📄 Full paper: https://lnkd.in/g5gzg_nh 

Movember / Southern Cross University / University of Melbourne / Orygen', 'Considering hiring a mental health professional to provide services to your athletes?  \xa0Since the term “sport psychology” is applied liberally to a vast field of professionals it is often difficult for those within the sports industry to understand whether they are working with someone who is licensed to practice psychology and can diagnose and treat mental health problems, or someone trained to apply mental-preparation techniques to enhance athletic performance.  Here are some important points:

- For mental health (which also improves mental performance) hire full-time, part-time or as a consultant a licensed mental health professional for referrals and consultation. These professionals are often licensed to practice psychology, typically with a PhD in clinical or counseling psychology, or a masters-level clinician with a degree in counseling or social work; trained to identify, diagnose, treat mental-health problems; and have additional experience in athletics or with athletes.

- If you hire a "sports phycologist" - someone trained to apply mental-preparation techniques to enhance athletic performance only (e.g. PhD in Kinesiology), look for additional professional competency such as Certiﬁed Mental Performance Consultants® (CMPCs).  Just remember these individuals should be trained in how to refer to your mental health provider.

- Check the individual's credentials and licensure within your State to make sure they care for patients/clients/athletes.  This is a State-by-State licensure profession.  This includes telehealth visits in most states as well as "fly in" consultants.

- For example, in the State of Maryland, an individual can not call themselves as sports psychologist without a license to practice psychology.  This is not the same in every State.  Be wary, know who you are hiring.  

- Many professionals in this space are cash only and do not accept insurance so have a plan and put it in writing.  

- Know the rules.  For NCAA sports, actions of an individual that could make them a "countable coach" include actions such as shagging balls, assisting with drills, throwing batting practice, signaling in plays, serving as a practice player, setting up offense and defense alignments, or walking out to the pitching mound for player consultation during a game.

- A “non-coaching” sports psychologist might be allowed at practice or sit on the bench of competitions as long as they are simply observing and not performing coaching activities. 

- Develop and rehearse a Mental Health Emergency Action Plan.

- Free National Suicide Prevention & Crisis Lifeline - Text or Call 988 - available 24 hours a day.']

Name: 14_participation_prevention_analysis_initiative
Representative_Docs:
['Today is World Health Day. 

JA PRISM is working to strengthen prevention-oriented and rights-based approaches to mental health. By scaling and adapting 3 practices to new contexts, JA PRISM will help create\xa0supportive environments across Europe.
The project’s overall ambition: to improve mental health and wellbeing among Europe’s most\xa0vulnerable populations.
 #JAPRISM #EU4Health #HealthUnion #MentalHealth

https://lnkd.in/e-sepQJF', 'This is a super important piece of work — thanks so much for putting this analysis together. It is very much in line with what we are also increasingly seeing across the WHO Regional Office for Europe through our own 2ndEuropean Health Equity Status Report initiative - #HESRi2 data and analysis.
We will be launching the new WHO Europe Health Equity Status Report (HESRi2) this September — stay tuned.

One of the clearest emerging messages is that young people’s mental health, participation in education and employment, social protection, life opportunities and future resilience cannot be addressed in isolation from one another.

The findings also reinforce the urgent need for earlier intervention and prevention, alongside more integrated and practical support systems that help young people experiencing mental ill health remain connected to learning, participation and decent work.', 'As highlighted in recent OECD reports on adolescent wellbeing, mental health must be at the centre of prevention programmes for young people.

The European Prevention Curriculum (EUPC) also reminds us that effective prevention is not simply about substances themselves, but about strengthening protective factors in people’s lives.

Learning to live with others, accept frustrations, know ones emetions and learn how to regulate, manage difficulties, and build one’s own pathway are essential elements of positive socialisation. These experiences help adolescents develop resilience, autonomy, and a sense of purpose — key foundations for healthy and fulfilling lives.

Prevention is about creating the conditions for young people to grow into adults who can face challenges, build healthy relationships, and move forward without relying on harmful coping mechanisms. 

Investing in mental wellbeing means investing in stronger, healthier, and more confident generations.
João Vian Rubén Rodríguez-Cano Joella Anupol Bettina Kehr']

Name: 15_stigma_barriers_center_local
Representative_Docs:
["May is Mental Health Awareness Month. For many people — myself included — living with mental health challenges can feel like a silent battle. At times, seeking and receiving support feels possible. But more often, it can feel out of reach. Stigma, financial barriers, isolation, and limited access to care are only a few of the obstacles that so many know all too well.

In my role at HealthRight International, my motivation for strengthening mental health care systems begins as a personal one, but it's fueled knowing that so many others face similar struggles. I will always advocate that mental health needs to be approached from a community-based perspective and integrated with all aspects of health. How am I doing this? It starts with meeting communities where they are. But the true action is actually shifting the paradigm of how we think about mental health care: breaking down stigma through psychoeducation, training trusted members in the community in delivering low-intensity interventions, and developing sustainable financial models beyond Medicaid billing that make both accessing and providing care feasible over the long term.

If you're passionate about mental health, let's connect.", 'I’m wearing green this month for Mental Health Awareness Month because it’s a good reminder to check in on each other.

Each year in the U.S., 20% of adults and 17% of adolescents experience a mental illness, and those needs continue to grow in both complexity and urgency. At HealthPartners, we are focused on closing the gap between demand and access.

One way we are doing that is through a rapid access model that brings behavioral health expertise directly into primary and specialty care visits. When we can address needs earlier, we reduce barriers for patients, shorten wait times for ongoing therapy, and improve access across the system.

We’re also addressing stigma, which remains one of the most persistent barriers to care. Through our Make It OK campaign, we’re helping normalize conversations about mental health and supporting more open dialogue in our communities.

Help us end the stigma: https://makeitok.org/

Our commitment to supporting mental wellness for those we serve is a priority year-round, not just in May.', 'As we near the end of Mental Health Awareness Month, I’d like to reflect on a cause that I care deeply about.\xa0
\xa0
After we moved our family from Houston to Boston about 8 years ago, my wife, Masu, and I got involved with The Brookline Center for Community Mental Health, a local resource that provides affordable mental health care and community-based social services to individuals and families in Massachusetts. Years later, my wife now serves on the Center’s board as its strategic development chair\xa0and we have chaired their Kinds Fund gala, deepening our connection to the Center and its mission.\xa0
\xa0
My wife and I felt called to support the need for accessible mental health care following the pandemic. As parents, we saw firsthand how isolation and the loss of connection greatly impacted social development and relationship building, especially among young people.\xa0
\xa0
While this month helps raise awareness, the responsibility to listen, support one another, and build stronger communities extends far beyond May. I’m proud to support the critical, lifesaving care the Brookline Center provides in my local community, and to bring that same commitment to EVgo by supporting the wellbeing of our team through employee mental health initiatives.\xa0
\xa0
What causes are important to you?
\xa0
https://lnkd.in/gVhyRzGz\xa0\xa0
\xa0
#MentalHealthAwareness #Community #EVgo']

Name: 16_region_2026_12_april
Representative_Docs:
['Mental Health Leadership Advocacy Course

📌Funding Partners: Africa CDC\xa0
📌Deadline: 31-Jan-2026

Description

Africa CDC Mental Health Leadership Programme (MHLP) will sponsor public health professionals, mental health enthusiast and persons with lived experience with innovative projects to strengthen mental health systems in Africa to attend the course in Harare, Zimbabwe.

MHLP is a three-year flagship programme aimed at preparing leaders\xa0to apply multifaceted approaches to understand and address mental health on the continent through a public health and human rights lens, to influence mental health policy, legislation and practice at all levels of the healthcare systems. 

For more information: https://lnkd.in/d2JipeVt

Follow us👇
https://lnkd.in/ebyetrNc
https://lnkd.in/eJZNcGyY', 'Mental health is a critical part of health and well-being and a fundamental human right. However, mental health conditions affect close to 150 million people in the region and many do not access the support and help they need. 

To support this area of health, the African Region is guided by the Regional framework to strengthen the implementation of the comprehensive mental health action plan 2013–2030 in the African Region. 

Join us for the the first webinar of the 2026 African Region Mental Health Webinar Series focusing on Accelerating Progress on the Regional Mental Health Framework in the African Region as we:
